task: Log-likelihood keyword extraction (node vs. reference corpus, explicit LL threshold and minimum frequency cut-off)
notable human research: Dunning, T. (1993). Accurate methods for the statistics of surprise and coincidence. Computational Linguistics, 19(1), 61–74.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Routine to compute manually given corpora and scripts.
Research Significance — Explanation: Foundational keyness method central to corpus linguistics.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: LLMs with tool use can execute LL keyness end-to-end today.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Near-complete automation expected with mature keyness toolchains.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: LL thresholds and frequency cut-offs are clearly specified in prose.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Papers routinely report LL threshold and min-freq values.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Deterministic given identical inputs and thresholds.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: AntConc, #LancsBox, CQPweb, R 'quanteda' all support it.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published keyword lists allow direct comparison.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Parameters (threshold, min-freq, reference corpus) sweep easily.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Low audit cost; counts and LL values reproducible.

task: BIC-based keyword ranking (BIC as association measure)
notable human research: Wilson, A. (2013). Embracing Bayes factors for key item analysis in corpus linguistics. In M. Bieswanger & A. Koll-Stobbe (Eds.), New approaches to the study of linguistic variability. Peter Lang.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Computationally straightforward but less common in practice.
Research Significance — Explanation: Emerging Bayesian alternative to LL for keyness.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: LLMs can compute BIC keyness with scripting support.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Fully automatable as BIC keyness matures.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: No theoretical barrier to full automation.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Wilson's specification is explicit and parseable.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: BIC and frequency cut-offs typically reported.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: R packages and quanteda support BIC-style ranking.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published BIC rankings allow direct verification.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Low verification cost; reproducible numeric output.

task: Multi-word keyword / n-gram keyness (bigrams or trigrams)
notable human research: O'Donnell, M. B. (2011). The adjusted frequency list: A method to produce cluster-sensitive frequency lists. ICAME Journal, 35, 135–169.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: N-gram keyness is tractable but data-heavy for humans.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can extract and rank n-gram keyness reliably.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Method prose generally specifies n-gram size and measure.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Min frequency and n are commonly reported.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: quanteda, NLTK, AntConc handle n-gram keyness.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Moderate judgement in choosing n and cut-offs.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published n-gram lists enable ground-truth comparison.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Easy to vary n and thresholds systematically.

task: Semantic category keyword clustering (e.g., post-hoc USAS tagging)
notable human research: Rayson, P. (2008). From key words to key semantic domains. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 13(4), 519–549.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Manual USAS post-hoc tagging is tedious.
Research Significance — Explanation: Valuable for semantic interpretation of keywords.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can run USAS via Wmatrix API with oversight.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Substantially automatable with better semantic taggers.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Automatable though tagger accuracy bounds replicability.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Category schemes are documented but mappings vary.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Papers often omit exact tagger version/settings.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Tagger outputs mostly deterministic but version-dependent.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Interpretation of semantic categories needs judgement.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published category counts verifiable if tagger matches.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Can vary tagger thresholds and granularity.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Moderate audit cost due to semantic categorisation.

task: Diachronic keyword comparison (keyness across two time periods)
notable human research: Baker, P. (2011). Times may change, but we will always have money: Diachronic variation in recent British English. Journal of English Linguistics, 39(1), 65–88.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Diachronic keyness doable but requires careful corpus splits.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can operationalise diachronic LL comparisons.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Specifications usually clear on time windows and measure.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Time slices and thresholds typically reported.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Standard corpus tools support diachronic keyness.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published diachronic keyword lists support verification.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Period boundaries and thresholds easily varied.

task: MI-based collocation with fixed window (±3 span, explicit frequency threshold)
notable human research: Church, K. W., & Hanks, P. (1990). Word association norms, mutual information, and lexicography. Computational Linguistics, 16(1), 22–29.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: MI collocation is a standard manual task.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI handles MI collocation trivially today.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Window and frequency thresholds clearly specified.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Papers typically report span and min-freq.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: AntConc, #LancsBox, Sketch Engine all support MI.

task: Log-Dice collocation ranking (±5 span)
notable human research: Rychlý, P. (2008). A lexicographer-friendly association score. In Proceedings of RASLAN 2008 (pp. 6–9). Masaryk University.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Log-Dice is a routine collocation computation.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can compute Log-Dice end-to-end.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Formula and span are standardly documented.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Collocate rankings verifiable against published output.

task: Asymmetric window collocation (e.g., L3R1 non-symmetric span)
notable human research: Brezina, V., McEnery, T., & Wattam, S. (2015). Collocations in context: A new perspective on collocation networks. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 20(2), 139–173.

Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: CQPweb and custom scripts handle asymmetric spans.

task: Collocational profile comparison across registers

Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can automate comparison with oversight.

task: Second-order collocation (collocation of collocates)
notable human research: Williams, G. (1998). Collocational networks: Interlocking patterns of lexis in a corpus of plant biology research articles. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 3(1), 151–171.

Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can implement with guidance.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: No canonical tool; custom code needed.

task: DP-based dispersion analysis (Gries's Deviation of Proportions)
notable human research: Gries, S. Th. (2008). Dispersions and adjusted frequencies in corpora. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 13(4), 403–437.

Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can compute DP reliably.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Part sizes and corpus splits typically reported.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: R packages and Gries's scripts implement DP.

task: Juilland's D comparison across registers
notable human research: Juilland, A., & Chang-Rodriguez, E. (1964). Frequency dictionary of Spanish words. Mouton.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Juilland's D is a standard calculation.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can compute Juilland's D directly.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Available in R and Python corpus packages.

task: Frequency vs. dispersion trade-off mapping
notable human research: Gries, S. Th. (2020). Analyzing dispersion. In M. Paquot & S. Th. Gries (Eds.), A practical handbook of corpus linguistics. Springer.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can generate the maps with oversight.

task: CQL/CQP concordance search with explicit query string
notable human research: Evert, S., & Hardie, A. (2011). Twenty-first century Corpus Workbench: Updating a query architecture for the new millennium. In Proceedings of the Corpus Linguistics 2011 Conference. University of Birmingham.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: CQL queries are routine for trained analysts.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Modern LLMs can translate prose to CQL.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: CQPweb, Sketch Engine, NoSketch Engine mature.

task: Lexico-grammatical pattern extraction via dependency parsing (SVO triples)
notable human research: Mason, O., & Hunston, S. (2004). The automatic recognition of verb patterns: A feasibility study. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 9(2), 253–270.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can run parsing pipelines competently.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Specifications often name parser and rules.

task: Constructional frequency profiling (syntactic construction counts across genres)
notable human research: Stefanowitsch, A. (2013). Collostructional analysis. In T. Hoffmann & G. Trousdale (Eds.), The Oxford handbook of construction grammar. Oxford University Press.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI with guidance can approximate counts.

task: Semantic prosody assessment via concordance sampling
notable human research: Louw, B. (1993). Irony in the text or insincerity in the writer? The diagnostic potential of semantic prosodies. In M. Baker, G. Francis, & E. Tognini-Bonelli (Eds.), Text and technology (pp. 157–176). John Benjamins.

Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI struggles with nuanced prosody judgements.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Concordancers exist but judgement remains human.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: No objective ground truth for prosody.

task: Discourse preference profiling (co-occurrence of evaluative lexis around a target)
notable human research: Partington, A., Duguid, A., & Taylor, C. (2013). Patterns and meanings in discourse: Theory and practice in corpus-assisted discourse studies (CADS). John Benjamins.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI gives partial support for co-occurrence mining.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Upper bound limited by interpretive coding.

task: Overuse/underuse analysis in learner corpus (%DIFF or odds ratio vs. native reference)
notable human research: Granger, S. (1998). The computer learner corpus: A versatile new source of data for SLA research. In S. Granger (Ed.), Learner English on computer (pp. 3–18). Addison Wesley Longman.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can compute %DIFF/odds-ratio directly.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: CIA tools and R packages support it.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Some judgement on reference corpus choice.

task: Error-tagged frequency profiling (tagged error categories across L1 groups)
notable human research: Dagneaux, E., Denness, S., & Granger, S. (1998). Computer-aided error analysis. System, 26(2), 163–174.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI tagging still imperfect for errors.

task: Burrows's Delta authorship attribution (most-frequent-word profiles)
notable human research: Burrows, J. (2002). 'Delta': A measure of stylistic difference and a guide to likely authorship. Literary and Linguistic Computing, 17(3), 267–287.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI runs Delta robustly.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: stylo R package and others mature.

task: Foregrounding frequency profiling (statistically deviant lexical choices vs. genre norm)
notable human research: Stubbs, M. (2005). Conrad in the computer: Examples of quantitative stylistic methods. Language and Literature, 14(1), 5–24.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI support partial due to qualitative content.

task: COHA/COCA diachronic frequency trajectory (normalised frequency across decades)
notable human research: Davies, M. (2012). Expanding horizons in historical linguistics with the 400-million word Corpus of Historical American English. Corpora, 7(2), 121–157.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can pull trajectories via APIs.

task: Semantic change detection via co-occurrence shift
notable human research: Hamilton, W. L., Leskovec, J., & Jurafsky, D. (2016). Diachronic word embeddings reveal statistical laws of semantic change. In Proceedings of ACL 2016.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Manual detection is difficult across large corpora.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI embeddings partially capture change.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: SemEval benchmarks provide some ground truth.

task: Biber-style factor score computation (weighted linguistic feature counts)
notable human research: Biber, D. (1988). Variation across speech and writing. Cambridge University Press.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Biber MD is laborious by hand.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI pipelines can compute factor scores.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: MAT tool and Nini's MFTE mature.

task: Register comparison via MD dimension scores (e.g., Dimension 1: Involved vs. Informational)
notable human research: Biber, D., & Conrad, S. (2009). Register, genre, and style. Cambridge University Press.

Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI handles it with existing pipelines.

task: Filled pause frequency profiling (uh/um tokens per 1000 words across speaker groups)
notable human research: Tottie, G. (2011). Uh and um as sociolinguistic markers in British English. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 16(2), 173–197.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can extract filled pauses from transcripts.

task: Turn-taking pattern frequency analysis (overlap and latency distributions)

Human Feasibility — Explanation: Manual analysis of overlap/latency is slow.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI support limited without aligned audio.

task: Dictionary-based sentiment frequency analysis (LIWC or similar fixed lexicon)
notable human research: Pennebaker, J. W., Boyd, R. L., Jordan, K., & Blackburn, K. (2015). The development and psychometric properties of LIWC2015. University of Texas at Austin.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI runs LIWC-style counts easily.

task: Epistemic stance marker frequency comparison (hedges and boosters across genres)
notable human research: Hyland, K. (1998). Hedging in scientific research articles. John Benjamins.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can apply lists to corpora.

task: Lexical bundle extraction (Biber-style)

Research Significance — Explanation: Biber's method is standard in phraseology.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI replicates bundles well.

task: Phraseological frame profiling (p-frames / skipgrams)
notable human research: Gray, B., & Biber, D. (2013). Lexical frames in academic prose and conversation. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 18(1), 109–136.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can compute p-frames/skipgrams.

task: Colligation profiling
notable human research: Hoey, M. (2005). Lexical priming: A new theory of words and language. Routledge.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Colligation analysis requires parsing plus stats.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: AI can script pipelines.

task: Translation universals testing (simplification, explicitation, normalisation)

task: TTR and MTLD comparison across text types
notable human research: McCarthy, P. M., & Jarvis, S. (2010). MTLD, vocd-D, and HD-D: A validation study of sophisticated approaches to lexical diversity assessment. Behavior Research Methods, 42(2), 381–392.

task: MIPVU metaphor identification on corpus data

task: LDA topic modelling for corpus exploration

task: Effect-size-based keyness meta-analysis (Log Ratio / %DIFF)
notable human research: Hardie, A. (2014). Log Ratio: An informal introduction [Blog post]. ESRC Centre for Corpus Approaches to Social Science (CASS).

task: Register-feature co-occurrence network analysis

notable human research: Aijmer, K., & Rühlemann, C. (Eds.). (2015). Corpus pragmatics: A handbook. Cambridge University Press.
